**Findings:**  
Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules and masses, including multiple right upper lobe opacities consistent with scarring or granulomas; left apical opacity likely representing fibrosis versus nodule/mass; no focal consolidation to suggest pneumonia; normal heart size without cardiomegaly; tortuous thoracic aorta suggestive of age-related changes but not acute pathology.

Bilateral lung findings most compatible with chronic disease (e.g., prior infection) rather than active infectious process such as tuberculosis given lack of infiltrates. Left-sided lesion may represent tumor recurrence if patient has history of malignancy. No evidence of pneumothorax. Thoracic spine degenerative changes noted.

l3_raw:
**Findings:**  
No focal airspace disease or pneumothorax identified bilaterally. Heart size is normal and mediastinal contours unremarkable with no acute cardiopulmonary abnormality detected. Degenerative changes of bilateral glenohumeral joints noted, including osteophytes; degenerative joint space narrowing present at both acromioclavicular articulations without evidence of subluxation or dislocation. No displaced fracture visualized within limits of resolution.  

**Impression:**  
Acute pulmonary process excluded by imaging study performed today. Chronic findings include mild left basilar scarring/atelectasis versus fibrosis, right midlung pleural thickening consistent with prior inflammation/infection (e.g., tuberculosis), chronic small-to-moderate volume loss from multiple rib fractures over time, and stable post-traumatic deformities involving bony structures throughout thoracic spine. Bilateral shoulder arthropathy likely secondary to trauma but may also reflect age-related wear-and-tear pattern given lack of significant change between exams.

**Findings:**  
Right lower lobe airspace disease with right pleural effusion and/or thickening; left basilar atelectasis or infiltrate versus scarring  

**Impression:**  
Small to moderate sized loculated fluid collection within the fissures of both lungs, likely representing chronic inflammatory changes (fissureal inflammation) rather than active infection given lack of associated air space consolidation. Right lung base opacity may represent pneumonia superimposed over adjacent subsegmental atelectatic change but is not definitively diagnostic without follow-up imaging if clinically indicated. Left-sided findings most consistent with prior post-inflammatory fibrosis/scarring. No acute cardiopulmonary abnormality identified.

**Findings:**  
No focal airspace disease or pneumothorax identified bilaterally. No pleural effusion seen. Cardiomediastinal silhouette within normal limits and no acute cardiopulmonary abnormality detected. Midline sternotomy with intact wires; left hemidiaphragm elevated, likely due to subpulmonic fluid collection versus eventration of diaphragmatic segment III/IV. Calcified granulomas noted at bilateral lung bases consistent with prior infection (e.g., tuberculosis). Aortic calcification present without evidence of dissection.  

**Impression:**  
Cardiac size is unremarkable. Left lower lobe pneumonia unlikely given lack of air space opacity but cannot be excluded by single view film alone if clinically indicated. Subdiaphragmatic lucency overlying posterior right eighth rib may represent bowel loops rather than free intraperitoneal gas unless patient has abdominal pain. Postoperative changes from median sternotomy remain unchanged since last imaging study performed more recently.

**Findings:**  
Right lower lobe airspace disease with right pleural effusion and/or atelectasis; left basilar opacity consistent with subsegmental atelectasis or scarring versus infiltrate; large hiatal hernia containing air-fluid level, stomach bubble above diaphragm; calcified granulomas within both lungs bilaterally without evidence of active infection; no acute cardiopulmonary abnormality identified except as described below.  

**Impression:**  
Large hiatal hernia replacing gastric fundus anteriorly overlying midline mediastinum to upper abdomen. Right lung base opacities may represent pneumonia/airspace consolidation vs. aspiration/infiltrates secondary to underlying emphysema/scars from prior tuberculosis treatment (prior TB therapy). No definite pneumothorax seen but small apical blebs present which could be related to chronic pulmonary changes rather than primary pathology given their location relative to apexes. Left hemidiaphragmatic elevation likely due to volume loss resulting from previous surgery. Small bilateral costophrenic angle blunting represents residual postoperative fluid collections that have resolved since last imaging study performed approximately one year ago.
